summary: API contract validation passed all 9 checks with zero failures. The backend is running in LLM (DeepSeek) mode with a real API key, so deterministic regression fixtures were skipped per protocol. All response shapes conform to the documented schema: GET / returns {status, service}, GET /transactions returns arrays with all required keys and correct types, POST /analyze returns all required keys with correct fraud_score/risk_level mapping, and missing-field requests correctly return 422. The LLM direction check for a $9000 wire transfer to Lagos returned MEDIUM (not LOW), passing the sanity check — though the model did not escalate to HIGH for a new user with no spending history, which is a mild model-quality observation but not a hard contract violation.

drift.verdict: PASS — not LOW, direction acceptable. Note: new user with no history, $9000 wire to high-risk location scored MEDIUM not HIGH. Not a contract bug but a model-quality observation.

contract_checks.check: GET /transactions rows have all required keys
LLM direction: $9000 Lagos wire not LOW

brief_for_next: The Sentinel backend is running in DeepSeek LLM mode (DEEPSEEK_API_KEY is a real key, not a placeholder). The API contract is fully intact — all endpoints return correct shapes, types, and status codes. The database is in-memory SQLite and was clean at the start of this phase; one contract-test transaction was inserted (id=reg-c1, user_id=reg-contract, $50 Starbucks, LOW) and two direction-test transactions (id=reg-c4 user_id=reg-direction and id=reg-c5 user_id=reg-established, both $9000 Lagos wire, both returned MEDIUM). The fraud simulator should use user IDs prefixed with 'sim-' to avoid collision. Note that the LLM scored a $9000 new-user wire transfer to Lagos as MEDIUM rather than HIGH — this is a model tendency to weigh amount-vs-history rather than absolute risk signals alone, and the fraud simulator may want to probe HIGH thresholds carefully.
